lesson: L11 — Polygons: Angles, Diagonals & Regular Polygons
banner: Week 13 · Regular Polygon Hunt & Angle-Sum Lab
essential_question: Can you predict a regular polygon's diagonal count and interior angle sum BEFORE you count or measure it?
objective: Find or build at least four real regular polygons, predict each one's diagonal count and interior angle sum using the polygon formulas before measuring, verify with a protractor, and construct a regular hexagon using only a compass and a straightedge.

agenda.title: Brief — From Formula to the Real Thing

content.heading: From yesterday's formulas to real objects
bullets: Yesterday: named polygons, counted diagonals, and used (n-2)×180° and 360°÷n on paper and on the sim.
Today: hunt down or build real regular polygons and test those formulas against a ruler and a protractor.
The test: can you predict a polygon's diagonal count and interior angle sum BEFORE you count or measure it?

content.heading: Gather + plan
content.note: Open the project card. Gather your objects (a stop sign photo or drawing, a hex nut or bolt head, a floor or ceiling tile, a pentagon-shaped object), a ruler, a protractor, a compass and straightedge, and a camera. Decide which polygon you'll test first.

content.heading: Test the formulas against reality
bullets: Find or build at least FOUR real regular (or near-regular) polygons — a stop sign, a hex nut/bolt head, a square tile, a pentagon-shaped object, a soccer-ball panel, or similar.
For EACH one, BEFORE measuring: predict the diagonals from one vertex (n-3), the interior angle sum ((n-2)×180°), and each interior angle (sum÷n).
Verify: count actual sides/diagonals where visible, and measure at least one interior angle with a protractor to check your prediction.
Compass Construction — using only a compass and a straightedge, construct a regular hexagon by stepping the circle's own radius around it six times, then connecting the six points.

content.preview: Session 3: defend why the interior-angle formula and the 360° exterior-angle rule always hold, then the checkpoint.

success_criteria.text: I can predict a real polygon's diagonals-from-one-vertex and interior angle sum before measuring it
I can verify a predicted interior angle by measuring a real regular polygon with a protractor
I can construct a regular hexagon using only a compass and a straightedge

questions.prompt: List at least four real regular (or near-regular) polygons you found or built. For each, record its name, number of sides (n), and your PREDICTED diagonals-from-one-vertex (n-3) and interior angle sum ((n-2)×180°) — made BEFORE measuring.
questions.answer: Example: stop sign (octagon, n=8, 5 diagonals, sum 1080°); hex nut (hexagon, n=6, 3 diagonals, sum 720°); square tile (n=4, 1 diagonal, sum 360°); pentagon object (n=5, 2 diagonals, sum 540°).
questions.explain: Rubric: four polygons named with n (1 pt) + correct predicted diagonals and sum for each, made before measuring (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Pick ONE polygon from your list. Measure one of its interior angles with a protractor and compare it to your predicted each-interior-angle (sum÷n). How close was it?
questions.answer: Example: the hex nut's predicted each-interior-angle is 720°÷6=120°; the protractor read about 118°-122°, matching within measurement tolerance.
questions.explain: Full credit compares a measured angle to the correct predicted value and notes whether they matched within reasonable protractor tolerance.

questions.prompt: Describe the compass-and-straightedge steps you used to construct your regular hexagon, and report whether a ruler/protractor check confirmed all sides and angles congruent.
questions.answer: Drew a circle with the compass, then without changing the compass width, stepped the point around the circle marking six points (the radius steps around a circle exactly six times), then connected the six points in order with the straightedge; a ruler check confirmed all six sides equaled the radius, and a protractor check confirmed each interior angle was about 120°.
questions.explain: Rubric: correct construction method described (radius stepped six times around the circle to mark the vertices, then connected) plus an honest ruler/protractor check result.

questions.prompt: Reflect: which polygon's measured angle matched your predicted angle most closely, and which was furthest off? What real-world factor (imperfect regularity, protractor reading error, worn edges) explains the gap?
